parentQuestion: Select the correct statement regarding epithelia.

scaffolds.q: What does 'stratified' mean in epithelial classification?
options: Cells are arranged in multiple layers
scaffolds.explain: STRATIFIED means arranged in MULTIPLE LAYERS (strata). Stratified epithelium has cells stacked on top of each other, unlike simple epithelium which has only one layer.

wrongExplains.0: Flat cells are squamous; stratified refers to layering.
wrongExplains.2: Glandular epithelium specializes in secretion; stratified refers to layers.
wrongExplains.3: Transitional epithelium changes shape; stratified refers to layers.

scaffolds.q: What is the main advantage of having multiple epithelial layers?
options: Better protection against abrasion and damage
scaffolds.explain: Multiple layers provide BETTER PROTECTION against ABRASION and damage. If surface cells are damaged, deeper layers remain to protect underlying tissues.

wrongExplains.0: Multiple layers actually impair absorption by creating barriers.
wrongExplains.2: Multiple layers slow diffusion; simple epithelium is better for diffusion.

scaffolds.q: Where is stratified epithelium typically found?
options: Areas subject to friction and mechanical stress
scaffolds.explain: Stratified epithelium is found in areas subject to FRICTION AND MECHANICAL STRESS — skin, mouth, esophagus, vagina. Multiple layers protect against wear.

wrongExplains.0: Gas exchange needs thin simple squamous epithelium.
wrongExplains.2: Absorption needs simple epithelium to minimize barrier.
wrongExplains.3: Filtering surfaces have simple squamous for thinness.

options: By the shape of the basal (bottom) cells
By the shape of the apical (surface) cells
By the total number of layers
By the location in the body
scaffolds.explain: Stratified epithelium is named by the shape of the APICAL (SURFACE) cells. For example, stratified squamous has flat cells at the surface, even if deeper cells are cuboidal.

wrongExplains.0: Basal cells in stratified epithelium are often cuboidal regardless of type.
wrongExplains.2: The number of layers isn't used for naming.
wrongExplains.3: Classification is by cell shape, not location.

scaffolds.q: What types of cells make up stratified squamous epithelium?
options: Cuboidal/columnar cells at base, squamous cells at surface
Squamous cells at base, cuboidal at surface
scaffolds.explain: Stratified squamous has CUBOIDAL/COLUMNAR cells at the BASE (where they divide) and SQUAMOUS cells at the SURFACE. Cells flatten as they move toward the surface.

wrongExplains.0: Only the surface cells are squamous; basal cells are cuboidal.
wrongExplains.2: It's the opposite: Cuboidal at base, squamous at surface.
wrongExplains.3: The arrangement is organized, not random.

scaffolds.q: Which epithelium regenerates from the basal layer?
scaffolds.explain: STRATIFIED EPITHELIUM regenerates from the BASAL LAYER. Stem cells at the base divide, and daughter cells move upward, flattening as they reach the surface.

wrongExplains.0: Simple squamous regenerates from all cells, not a distinct basal layer.
wrongExplains.2: All epithelia can regenerate, not just transitional.

scaffolds.q: What happens to cells as they move from base to surface in stratified epithelium?
options: They become more specialized for absorption
scaffolds.explain: As cells move from base to surface, they FLATTEN and may DIE (keratinize). In keratinized epithelium like skin, surface cells are dead and filled with keratin protein.

wrongExplains.0: Cells flatten, not get rounder, as they move toward surface.
wrongExplains.2: Cell division occurs at the base, not surface.
wrongExplains.3: Surface cells are for protection, not absorption.

scaffolds.q: Why is absorption NOT a function of stratified epithelium?
options: Multiple layers create a barrier that blocks absorption
Only simple epithelium has a blood supply
scaffolds.explain: MULTIPLE LAYERS create a BARRIER that BLOCKS absorption. Substances would have to pass through many cell layers to reach underlying blood vessels — too slow and inefficient.

wrongExplains.0: Cell size isn't the issue; the number of layers is.
wrongExplains.2: Stratified cells have organelles; the barrier is the problem.
wrongExplains.3: Both types are avascular; simple epithelium is thinner for diffusion.

scaffolds.q: Select the correct statement regarding epithelia:
options: Simple epithelium is best for protection
Stratified epithelium is best for areas subject to abrasion
Stratified epithelium is best for absorption
Simple epithelium provides the most protection
scaffolds.explain: STRATIFIED EPITHELIUM is best for areas subject to ABRASION because multiple layers provide protection. Simple epithelium is thin for absorption/diffusion but offers less protection.

wrongExplains.0: Simple epithelium is too thin for protection; it's for exchange.
wrongExplains.2: Stratified epithelium blocks absorption; simple allows it.
